# tokens: # Behavior dispatch spec — covers `compare` and `canon` capabilities
# installed via the unified `behave` word.
#   behave compare/q  (fn [[T T] [Integer] [body]])
#   behave canon/q    (fn [[T]   [String]  [body]])
#   behave nodify/q  (fn [[T]   [Any]     [body]])
# `nodify` projects a value into a Node or Scalar (the AQL data
# subset) rather than into a JSON string — callers compose with a
# separate encoder when they need wire-format JSON. The default
# (when no nodify is registered) returns the value unchanged.
# Three dispatch layers:
#   1. CORE comparators / canonisers — kernel scalar Behaviors
#      (Integer, Decimal, Number cross-promotion, String, Boolean,
#      Atom) hold the canonical `Compare` and `Format` impls.
#   2. NATIVE comparators / canonisers — lang-layer domain types
#      (Date, Instant, ClkDuration, …) attach Compare / Format to
#      their existing FormatBehavior in lang/engine/native_temporal.go.
#   3. USER-DEFINED — `behave compare/q`, `behave canon/q`, and
#      `behave nodify/q` install AQL bodies via
#      lang/engine/native_behave.go's userBehavior wrapper. All three
#      capability slots coexist on one wrapper.
# 1a. CORE compare: kernel scalar Comparers
# Integer ordering — numberCompareBehavior on TNumber handles
# Integer-Integer pairs via the lattice walk plus Decimal pairs.
2 5 lt
5 2 lt
5 5 lt
5 5 lte
5 5 gte
5 2 gt
# Decimal ordering — same dispatch path, AsNumber coerces.
# Cross-type Integer + Decimal: LCA = Number; the Comparer on Number
# handles the entire numeric branch — no per-pair Behavior.
3.0 3 lte
# String lexicographic ordering.
'apple' 'banana' lt
'banana' 'apple' lt
'same' 'same' lt
# Boolean: false < true.
false true lt
true false lt
true true lt
# Atom: lex order by name.
red/q green/q lt
green/q red/q lt
red/q red/q lt
# Cross-branch (Integer vs String) — no shared Comparer below the
# root, so dispatch errors rather than silently coercing.
1 'a' lt
1 true lt
# 1b. CORE canon: kernel default rendering through CanonValue
# Each scalar value renders to its canonical AQL source form (the
# round-trippable representation produced by eng.CanonValue).
'hello'
true
false
red/q
# 2a. NATIVE compare: predefined domain Comparers
# Constructed via the aql:time module — the test runner pre-installs
# its exports so rows can use `time.unix`, `time.seconds`, … directly.
(0 time.unix) (100 time.unix) lt
(100 time.unix) (0 time.unix) lt
(50 time.unix) (50 time.unix) lt
(50 time.unix) (50 time.unix) lte
(0 time.unix) (100 time.unix) gt
(5 time.seconds) (10 time.seconds) lt
(10 time.seconds) (5 time.seconds) lt
# 2b. NATIVE canon: domain Format Behaviors
# Each native type's FormatBehavior produces a domain-specific render
# (Instant in RFC3339Nano, ClkDuration as a Go duration string, …).
0 time.unix
100 time.unix
5 time.seconds
# 3a. USER-DEFINED compare: behave compare/q (fn [[T T] [Integer] [body]])
# `behave compare/q` installs a Comparer on type T. The body receives the
# operands as the defs `a` and `b`; the trailing Integer determines
# ordering: negative → a<b, zero → equal, positive → a>b.
# Ascending by age.
type Person object {age:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Person Person] [Integer] [(a 'age' get) (b 'age' get) sub]]) def alice (make Person {age:30}) def bob (make Person {age:25}) alice bob lt
type Person object {age:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Person Person] [Integer] [(a 'age' get) (b 'age' get) sub]]) def alice (make Person {age:30}) def bob (make Person {age:25}) bob alice lt
type Person object {age:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Person Person] [Integer] [(a 'age' get) (b 'age' get) sub]]) def alice (make Person {age:30}) def bob (make Person {age:25}) alice bob gt
# Descending: swap operands.
type Item object {n:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Item Item] [Integer] [(b 'n' get) (a 'n' get) sub]]) def i1 (make Item {n:1}) def i9 (make Item {n:9}) i1 i9 lt
type Item object {n:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Item Item] [Integer] [(b 'n' get) (a 'n' get) sub]]) def i1 (make Item {n:1}) def i9 (make Item {n:9}) i9 i1 lt
# Self-equality.
type Person object {age:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Person Person] [Integer] [(a 'age' get) (b 'age' get) sub]]) def alice (make Person {age:30}) alice alice lt
type Person object {age:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Person Person] [Integer] [(a 'age' get) (b 'age' get) sub]]) def alice (make Person {age:30}) alice alice lte
# Sig validation: heterogeneous input params rejected for compare.
type Person object {age:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Person Integer] [Integer] [42]])
expected: ERROR:both params must be the same type

# tokens: # Return type validation.
type Person object {age:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Person Person] [String] ['oops']])
# Builtin type override refused.
behave compare/q (fn [[Integer Integer] [Integer] [0]])
# Unknown behavior name.
behave bogus/q (fn [[Integer Integer] [Integer] [0]])
# Body error propagates through the Comparer chain.
type Faulty object {x:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Faulty Faulty] [Integer] [quux]]) def v1 (make Faulty {x:1}) def v2 (make Faulty {x:2}) v1 v2 lt
# 3b. USER-DEFINED canon: behave canon/q (fn [[T] [String] [body]])
# `behave canon/q` installs a Format-via-canon Behavior on T. The body
# receives the value as the def `a` and returns a String — that
# String becomes the value's canonical render (via Value.String,
# eng.Canon, every error message).
# Render Person as just its name field. The body returns the raw
# string contents — that becomes the canonical render for Person
# values (Canon → CanonValue's default fallthrough → Value.String →
# userBehavior.Format → body output).
type Person object {name:String age:Integer} behave canon/q (fn [[Person] [String] [(a 'name' get)]]) make Person {name:'Alice' age:30}
# Multi-field canon body — string concat plus a fixed suffix.
type Person object {name:String age:Integer} behave canon/q (fn [[Person] [String] [(a 'name' get) ' (person)' add]]) make Person {name:'Alice' age:30}
# Canon coexists with compare on the same type — both wrap one
# userBehavior; installing the second does not lose the first.

type Person object {name:String age:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Person Person] [Integer] [(a 'age' get) (b 'age' get) sub]]) behave canon/q (fn [[Person] [String] [(a 'name' get)]]) def alice (make Person {name:'Alice' age:30}) def bob (make Person {name:'Bob' age:25}) alice bob lt
note: compare still works after canon installed

# tokens: type Person object {name:String age:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Person Person] [Integer] [(a 'age' get) (b 'age' get) sub]]) behave canon/q (fn [[Person] [String] [(a 'name' get)]]) make Person {name:'Alice' age:30}
note: canon still works after compare installed

# tokens: # Sig validation: canon must take exactly 1 param.
type Person object {name:String} behave canon/q (fn [[Person Person] [String] ['x']])
# Return-type validation.
type Person object {name:String} behave canon/q (fn [[Person] [Integer] [42]])
# 4. JSONIFY: project a value into a Node or Scalar
# `behave nodify/q (fn [[T] [Any] [body]])` installs a projection on T;
# the body's return value is a Node or Scalar (data-shape, NOT a
# JSON-encoded string). Two access words:
#   - `nodify VALUE` — runs the projection, returns the Node/Scalar
#     directly. Used here for spec assertions on the body output.
#   - `jsonify VALUE` — runs the projection THEN serialises via the
#     JSON encoder. Existing word — the customisation hook now flows
#     through it transparently.
# With no jsonify behavior registered for the type, `nodify` returns
# the value unchanged (Map / List / scalar are already data-shaped).
# 4a. Default pass-through for native data shapes.
5 nodify
'hello' nodify
true nodify
[1 2 3] nodify
{a:1 b:2} nodify
# 4b. NATIVE pass-through — domain types with no jsonify behavior
# attached also pass through unchanged.
0 time.unix nodify
# 4c. USER-DEFINED — a Person with a custom jsonify projects to a
# Map with renamed / filtered fields. The body returns a Map (Node);
# `nodify` exposes that Map directly so the spec can assert on its
# structure without going through the JSON-string encoder.
type Person object {name:String age:Integer secret:String} behave nodify/q (fn [[Person] [Any] [{n: (a 'name' get), age: (a 'age' get)}]]) make Person {name:'Alice' age:30 secret:'hush'} nodify
# Body can return Scalar, not just Node — projection is free to
# collapse the whole instance to a primitive.
type Money object {cents:Integer} behave nodify/q (fn [[Money] [Any] [(a 'cents' get)]]) make Money {cents:1234} nodify
# Compose with compare + canon on the same type — three capability
# slots coexist on one userBehavior wrapper.

type Item object {n:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Item Item] [Integer] [(a 'n' get) (b 'n' get) sub]]) behave canon/q (fn [[Item] [String] [(a 'n' get) ' (item)' add]]) behave nodify/q (fn [[Item] [Any] [{value: (a 'n' get)}]]) def i (make Item {n:42}) i nodify
# Sig validation — jsonify takes exactly 1 param.
type Foo object {x:Integer} behave nodify/q (fn [[Foo Foo] [Any] [42]])
# 5. PREDICATE-TYPE DISPATCH
# `type Positive fn [n:Integer Integer [(n gt 0) guard n]]` declares a
# refinement of Integer. InstallType mints Positive's *Type with
# parent=Integer (because PredicateInputType returns the concrete
# input type). `def x:Positive 5` validates via the predicate body
# and then rewraps x with VType=Positive — the carrier idea from
# BEHAVIORS.0.md.
# Result: behaviors installed on Positive dispatch on values
# bound through typed-def; the structural-vs-nominal gotcha is
# resolved for predicate types.
# Without a Positive Comparer installed, x y lt falls through to
# Integer's lattice ancestor (Number's Comparer) — preserves the
# default ordering.
type Positive fn [n:Integer Integer [(n gt 0) guard n]] def x:Positive 5 def y:Positive 10 x y lt
note: default numeric ordering inherited via Positive→Integer→Number

# tokens: # Installing Positive's Comparer (descending: `b a sub`) flips the
# result. This row is the canary: if the typed-def rewrap fails,
# Positive's Comparer is never found and lt stays at `true` (the
# Integer default). It returns `false` only when dispatch finds
# Positive's Comparer — proving the gotcha is closed.
type Positive fn [n:Integer Integer [(n gt 0) guard n]] behave compare/q (fn [[Positive Positive] [Integer] [b a sub]]) def x:Positive 5 def y:Positive 10 x y lt
note: Positive's descending Comparer wins → 5 lt 10 = false

# tokens: # typeof on a typed-bound value reports the asserted type — Positive
# in canonical form is just its leaf name.
type Positive fn [n:Integer Integer [(n gt 0) guard n]] def x:Positive 5 typeof x
# Validation gate still fires — a value failing the predicate body
# (here, -3 doesn't satisfy `n gt 0`) is rejected at bind time.
type Positive fn [n:Integer Integer [(n gt 0) guard n]] def x:Positive -3 x
# A Positive value still satisfies `is Integer` — the lattice walk
# (Positive→Integer) keeps the parent relationship intact, so
# downstream typed positions accepting Integer accept a Positive too.
type Positive fn [n:Integer Integer [(n gt 0) guard n]] def x:Positive 5 x is Integer
# Canon override works on a predicate type — body returns a String
# rendering.
type Positive fn [n:Integer Integer [(n gt 0) guard n]] behave canon/q (fn [[Positive] [String] ['+'  a add]]) def x:Positive 7 x
note: canon body produces a String render

# tokens: # 6. OBJECT-TYPE DISPATCH ON TYPED DEFS
# `def x:Person {map}` now builds a Person-typed ObjectInstance via
# make-style construction. The result carries VType=Person so
# behaviors installed on Person dispatch on x — closes the
# structural-vs-nominal gap for object types alongside the predicate
# fix above.
# Without a Person Comparer, two Persons can't be compared (the LCA
# walk from Person goes to TObjectType which has no Comparer).
type Person object {name:String age:Integer} def x:Person {name:'A' age:30} typeof x
note: def x:Person {map} now produces a Person-tagged instance

# tokens: # Person Comparer dispatches on values bound via typed def.
type Person object {name:String age:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Person Person] [Integer] [(a 'age' get) (b 'age' get) sub]]) def x:Person {name:'A' age:30} def y:Person {name:'B' age:25} x y lt
note: 30 lt 25 → false; Comparer wired through typed-def

# tokens: # `def x:Person make Person {map}` accepts a pre-made instance.
type Person object {name:String age:Integer} def x:Person make Person {name:'A' age:30} typeof x
note: pre-made ObjectInstance accepted by typed def

# tokens: # 7. SORT — uses Comparer dispatch
# `LIST sort` returns a new List sorted ascending using
# eng.CompareValues; `MAP sort` returns a new Map ordered by values.
# Pairs the lattice can't compare fall back to string-form ordering
# so sort stays total.
# Sort a list of integers.
# Sort a list of strings (lexical).
['banana' 'apple' 'cherry'] sort
# Already-sorted is a no-op.
[1 2 3] sort
# Sort uses the user-installed Comparer when one is registered.
# The list of Item instances comes back in `n`-ascending order.
type Item object {n:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Item Item] [Integer] [(a 'n' get) (b 'n' get) sub]]) [(make Item {n:5}) (make Item {n:1}) (make Item {n:3})] sort length
note: sort returns a list of three items

# tokens: # A nodify body can project Items to bare integers — we sort first
# (using Item's Comparer), then project to make the spec assertion
# easy. The order in the projected list confirms the Comparer fired.

type Item object {n:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Item Item] [Integer] [(a 'n' get) (b 'n' get) sub]]) behave nodify/q (fn [[Item] [Any] [(a 'n' get)]]) [(make Item {n:5}) (make Item {n:1}) (make Item {n:3})] sort each [nodify]
note: sort dispatches via Item's Comparer; nodify projects to ints

# tokens: # Descending: same Items, inverted body.

type Item object {n:Integer} behave compare/q (fn [[Item Item] [Integer] [(b 'n' get) (a 'n' get) sub]]) behave nodify/q (fn [[Item] [Any] [(a 'n' get)]]) [(make Item {n:5}) (make Item {n:1}) (make Item {n:3})] sort each [nodify]
# Sort a map by value — entries appear in ascending value order.
{c:3 a:1 b:2} sort
{z:10 y:5 x:1} sort
